envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Metal Kitchen Cookware, Utensil, Cutlery, and Flatware (except Precious) Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat production programs and purchase orders priced per item, set, lot, or private-label line, often including product development, tooling, finishing, packaging, compliance documentation, inventory, and fulfillment.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS code combines branded consumer-product manufacturers with contract, private-label, OEM, and institutional suppliers. These models have materially different customer recurrence and transfer economics, so the lens retains repeat outsourced and private-label suppliers and excludes primarily branded consumer businesses.
lens.excluded_activities: Primarily branded consumer-product companies without a recurring outsourced-manufacturing or private-label model, captive internal factories, pure importers and distributors, foundries, unfinished-stamping suppliers, precious-metal flatware, non-control financing vehicles, and owner-dependent craft makers without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying private-label, contract-manufactured, OEM, foodservice, hospitality, institutional, distributor, or retail-program metal cookware, kitchen utensils, nonprecious cutlery, and flatware for external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures all NAICS 332 fabricated-metal manufacturing, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in the narrowed 332215 lens. In May 2023, production occupations were 58.31% and transportation/material-moving occupations were 6.51% of NAICS 332 employment, with assemblers/fabricators alone at 10.84%. I assign low language-model exposure to forming, machining, welding, grinding, polishing, coating, plating, heat treatment, assembly, packing, maintenance, and physical inspection, but higher exposure to product and packaging specification work, RFQs, costing, demand and inventory planning, sourcing, quality records, customer service, and administration. Consumer-goods suppliers may have more product, merchandising, and account-management labor than the broad metal sector, so the proxy may understate exposure.
caveats: Occupation evidence is for all fabricated metal manufacturing, not 332215.
The narrowed lens may have a different domestic-versus-import and office-labor mix from the broad NAICS population.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task-time study across 10-15 US contract/private-label cookware and cutlery plants, split by product development, commercial, sourcing, production, quality, logistics, and administration and documenting current automation, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The manufacturing core is physical, but private-label and institutional programs generate document-heavy workflows around product specifications, packaging, samples, quotes, forecasts, purchase orders, sourcing, test results, claims, and customer communication.

caveats: No complete constant-price five-year forecast was found.
The narrowed outsourced/private-label subset may grow differently from the total consumer-product industry.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census shipments and unit data by cookware, utensil, cutlery, and flatware category, retailer scanner data, foodservice equipment purchases, hospitality openings, import volumes, and private-label program forecasts would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Household formation, replacement, restaurants, hospitality, institutional kitchens, gifting, and culinary interest support recurring physical demand, while long product lives, household saturation, imports, economic cycles, reuse, and material substitution restrain growth. Product-safety scrutiny may shift share among suppliers rather than eliminate the category.

caveats: The provided n uses receipts and a machinery-industry margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA.
Census reports 205 employer establishments in 2023, but not their business model or economic band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the 21 dataset-estimated candidates by branded versus private-label revenue, domestic manufacturing content, external repeat programs, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, and owner dependence would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: Only the contract, private-label, OEM, and repeat institutional portion fits the coherent outsourced lens. Branded consumer businesses, import/distribution-heavy firms, captive plants, craft makers, firms outside the true EBITDA band, and operations whose product identity or relationships are inseparable from the owner are excluded.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332215 ($816,870,918) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332215 ($3,341,473,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $449,848 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332215); class <5: 116 firms, avg revenue/firm $424,716 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $83,329 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,069,778 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $209,890 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,688,538 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $527,491 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,992,095 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,352,849 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,785,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,533,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $192,048,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $37,679,850 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 21

caveats: Domestic operators face import displacement even if US consumption persists.
Branded product marketing is excluded from the narrowed operator lens.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing interviews and lost-order data quantifying direct imports, retailer-owned sourcing, insourcing, alternative materials, rental/reuse models, and automated local production would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Remaining demand is for durable physical food-preparation and dining goods and requires accountable product engineering, material selection, fabrication, finishing, assembly, testing, packaging, and release. Software can support design and ordering but cannot supply the product; imports, customer direct sourcing, or insourcing can replace a domestic outsourced operator.

caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit.
Differentiated professional products and commodity private-label flatware have different pricing power.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, bid and line-review cadence, cost-breakdown requirements, price-adjustment clauses, customer concentration, quote win/loss data, and gross-margin bridges after productivity changes would establish actual retention.
q.rationale: Private-label and institutional programs can retain savings between line reviews, particularly when the supplier provides design, compliance, packaging, inventory, and reliable fulfillment. Retailers, distributors, large foodservice buyers, competitive imports, annual bids, cost breakdowns, and commodity price comparison create strong pass-through pressure.

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured five-year adoption rate.
Safety and food-contact evidence illustrates constraints but does not measure workflow implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Before/after deployments reporting sustained labor hours, development and quote cycle time, forecast error, stockouts, specification defects, claims, model exceptions, user adoption, and retailer/customer acceptance would materially update the range.
rho.rationale: Specification ingestion, assisted costing, packaging and label review, demand and purchase planning, supplier-document checks, test-record triage, claims classification, and customer service are implementable within five years. Legacy systems, SKU proliferation, retailer-specific data, product safety, food-contact judgment, image and brand risk, and physical sample approval limit realized implementation.

caveats: No comprehensive six-digit control-transfer series was found.
Brand-only deals, licensing, asset sales, and internal reorganizations must be excluded unless the operating firm transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-matched ten-year transaction set from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and consumer-products advisers, paired with owner-age and succession-plan data for the eligible firms, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: A small eligible population limits transaction frequency. Retirement and strategic/private-label consolidation can create sales, but brand entanglement, retailer concentration, offshore sourcing, product liability, family succession, and closures or asset sales prevent many qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Private-label and institutional programs create repeat specification, sourcing, planning, compliance, packaging, and customer workflows around durable physical products.
principal_weakness: The eligible firm pool is likely very small, and powerful buyers plus imports limit retention of productivity gains.
